item.title: The Billionaire’s Son Who Went on a "Test Drive "and Left Someone Dead
item.description: The Billionaire’s Son Who Went on a "Test Drive "and Left Someone Dead

media:content.media:title: The Billionaire’s Son Who Went on a "Test Drive "and Left Someone Dead
media:content.media:description: The Billionaire’s Son Who Went on a "Test Drive "and Left Someone Dead

item.title: Coerced To CONFESS The Truth
item.description: Title: Karen Boes Conviction Under Review After Two Decades in Prison Description: Karen Boes was convicted in 2003 of first-degree felony murder for a July 2002 house fire that killed her 14-year-old daughter, Robin, at their Zeeland, Michigan home. Prosecutors established the fire was intentionally set using an accelerant, and statements Boes made during a lengthy police interrogation were used as key evidence against her. She was sentenced to life in prison without the possibility of parole and has maintained her innocence throughout. In 2021, the Michigan Innocence Clinic at the University of Michigan Law School took up her case, arguing the conviction relied on fire investigation methods now considered scientifically unreliable and interrogation techniques experts say may have produced a false confession. The Michigan Court of Appeals ordered an evidentiary hearing in July 2024, finding the trial court had abused its discretion by denying that opportunity. Four days of testimony concluded in early 2026, with experts on both sides presenting conflicting accounts. A ruling on whether Boes will receive a new trial had not been issued as of early 2026. She remains incarcerated at a Michigan correctional facility.

media:content.media:title: Coerced To CONFESS The Truth
media:content.media:description: Title: Karen Boes Conviction Under Review After Two Decades in Prison Description: Karen Boes was convicted in 2003 of first-degree felony murder for a July 2002 house fire that killed her 14-year-old daughter, Robin, at their Zeeland, Michigan home. Prosecutors established the fire was intentionally set using an accelerant, and statements Boes made during a lengthy police interrogation were used as key evidence against her. She was sentenced to life in prison without the possibility of parole and has maintained her innocence throughout. In 2021, the Michigan Innocence Clinic at the University of Michigan Law School took up her case, arguing the conviction relied on fire investigation methods now considered scientifically unreliable and interrogation techniques experts say may have produced a false confession. The Michigan Court of Appeals ordered an evidentiary hearing in July 2024, finding the trial court had abused its discretion by denying that opportunity. Four days of testimony concluded in early 2026, with experts on both sides presenting conflicting accounts. A ruling on whether Boes will receive a new trial had not been issued as of early 2026. She remains incarcerated at a Michigan correctional facility.
